hints: Build the hand: pick the trip rank & suits, then two distinct other ranks each with one card.
solution:
Choose the rank of the triple ($13$) and $3$ of its $4$ suits ($\binom43=4$); choose $2$ other distinct ranks ($\binom{12}{2}=66$) and one suit for each ($4\cdot4$). So
$$P=\frac{13\cdot4\cdot66\cdot16}{\binom{52}{5}}=\frac{54912}{2598960}=\frac{88}{4165}.$$

hints: You'd only reflip the coins that came up tails.
Each coin ends heads w.p. ½ (already) + ½·½ (tails then reflip) = 3/4.
solution: Reflip exactly the tails. Per coin, P(final heads)$=\tfrac12+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac34$. By linearity over $8$ coins the fair value is $8\cdot\tfrac34=\$6.$

hints: Need XY>½ with X,Y∈(0,1); this forces X>½.
Bound the expected number of open length-n self-avoiding paths through the origin.
There are at most 4·3^{n−1} such paths, each open w.p. p^n; expectation ≤ 4·3^{n−1}p^n.
This →0 iff 3p<1, i.e. p<1/3, so d=1/3.
solution: If the origin is in an infinite cluster, then for every $n$ there is an open self-avoiding path of length $n$ from it. The number of self-avoiding paths of length $n$ starting at the origin is at most $4\cdot3^{n-1}$, and each is open with probability $p^n$. By sub-additivity (union bound), the probability such a path exists is $\le 4\cdot3^{n-1}p^n=\tfrac43(3p)^n$, which $\to0$ as $n\to\infty$ exactly when $3p<1$. Hence percolation probability is $0$ for $p\in(0,\tfrac13)$, so $d=\tfrac13.$

hints: A long butterfly peaks at the middle (body) strike.
Payoff is maximal when S_T equals the body strike.
solution: A long butterfly's payoff is a tent peaking at the body strike and zero outside the wings. It is maximized when the underlying finishes exactly at the body strike, so you want $S_T=37.5.$

hints: Each round: P1 wins on 1–2 (prob 1/3); else P2 wins on 3–6 (prob 2/3 of P2's roll); else repeat.
solution: In a round, P1 wins immediately w.p. $\tfrac13$. Otherwise (w.p. $\tfrac23$) P2 rolls and wins w.p. $\tfrac46=\tfrac23$; only if P2 also fails ($\tfrac13$) does the game restart. So $p_1=\tfrac13+\tfrac23\cdot\tfrac13\,p_1\Rightarrow p_1(1-\tfrac29)=\tfrac13\Rightarrow p_1=\tfrac37$, hence $p_2=\tfrac47$ and $\max=\tfrac47.$

hints: This is the camel–bananas problem: shuttle in phases; empty return trips spill nothing.
3 loads cost 3 gal/mi until 2000 left (333⅓ mi); 2 loads cost 2 gal/mi until 1000 (500 mi); then 1 gal/mi.
Distances 333⅓+500=833⅓ used, 166⅔ mi left on the last load ⇒ 1000−166⅔ ≈ 833.
solution:
Shuttle in phases (only loaded forward trips spill).
• With 3 loads (3000 gal) each mile needs $3$ forward passes $=3$ gal/mi; reduce to $2000$ over $1000/3=333\tfrac13$ mi.
• With 2 loads ($2000$): $2$ gal/mi; reduce to $1000$ over $1000/2=500$ mi.
• Total moved so far $=833\tfrac13$ mi, holding $1000$ gal; with a single load it's $1$ gal/mi for the remaining $166\tfrac23$ mi.
Arrive with $1000-166\tfrac23\approx 833$ gallons.

hints: Adding a regressor can never lower R², so the joint R² ≥ the larger single one (0.2).
It can be as high as 1. So [min,max]=[0.2,1].
solution: Including more regressors never decreases $R^2$, so regressing on both gives $R^2\ge\max(0.15,0.2)=0.2$; the upper limit is $1$. Thus $[\min,\max]=[0.2,1]$ and $\dfrac{\max}{\min}=\dfrac{1}{0.2}=5.$

hints: σ=√4=2; standardize.
First update which bowl you hold given a blue draw, then compute the next blue probability.
P(blue)=½; posteriors P(BB|blue)=2/3, P(mixed|blue)=1/3.
Next blue (with replacement)=2/3·1 + 1/3·½ = 5/6.
solution: $P(\text{first blue})=\tfrac13(1)+\tfrac13(0)+\tfrac13(\tfrac12)=\tfrac12$. Posteriors: $P(\text{BB}\mid\text{blue})=\tfrac{1/3}{1/2}=\tfrac23$, $P(\text{mixed}\mid\text{blue})=\tfrac{1/6}{1/2}=\tfrac13$. With replacement the next draw is blue with probability $\tfrac23\cdot1+\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac56.$

hints: Gamma measures curvature of the option price in S.
Deep OTM, the price is ~flat (delta≈0 and barely changing).
So gamma ≈ 0.
solution: Gamma is the curvature of the option value in the underlying. Far out-of-the-money, the call is nearly worthless and its delta stays near $0$ for small moves, so the value is essentially flat — gamma $\approx 0.$

hints: The arc covering a fixed point is size-biased: longer arcs are likelier to contain it.
For n points splitting a circle of circumference L, the arc containing a fixed point has mean L·2/(n+1).
solution: With $3$ uniform points the circle splits into $3$ arcs (spacings). A fixed point like $(1,0)$ is more likely to fall in a longer arc, so its arc is size-biased. For $n$ uniform points on a circle of circumference $L$, the expected length of the arc containing a fixed point is $L\cdot\dfrac{2}{n+1}$. Here $n=3,\,L=2\pi$, giving $2\pi\cdot\tfrac{2}{4}=\pi$, so $q=1.$
